takeaways: Senior housing approaches 90% occupancy industry-wide as oldest boomers turn 80 — same-store rent growth holds above 4.3% YoY while new supply hits a two-decade low.
Nearly $1T in CRE loans mature in 2026 with average borrowing costs at 6.57% vs. 6.34% cap rates — Colliers forecasts 15–20% sales volume rebound as capital re-enters.

Agentic AI moves from pilot to operational dependence in proptech; market projected to triple from $51.7B in 2026 to $139B by 2033 as operators deploy autonomous procurement, pricing, and maintenance agents.

items.headline: Senior living industry hits 90% occupancy as boomer wave arrives
items.summary: Industry-wide occupancy is approaching 90% in 2026, improving roughly 50 basis points per quarter, with the oldest baby boomers turning 80 this year driving record demand. Year-over-year inventory growth is at its lowest level since 2006, creating a structural supply-demand imbalance favorable to Lloyd Jones senior housing assets.

items.headline: Same-store senior housing rents grow 4.3% as discounts vanish
items.summary: Asking rent growth has stabilized above 4% YoY with same-store rents up 4.3% in Q3 2025, and PERE reports operator discounts will disappear in 2026 as demand outstrips supply. Strong pricing power supports underwriting of higher NOI growth for Lloyd Jones senior housing acquisitions.

items.headline: Nearly $1T in CRE loans mature in 2026, sparking refi wave
items.summary: Roughly $1 trillion in commercial real estate loans come due in 2026, with over $560B in transaction volume returning to the market. Borrowing costs hover at 6.57% against average cap rates of 6.34% — an exceptionally tight spread that pressures highly levered owners and creates acquisition opportunities for well-capitalized sponsors.

items.headline: Colliers forecasts 15-20% CRE sales volume surge in 2026
items.summary: Capital is flowing again with institutional and cross-border buyers re-entering the market, and Colliers projects 15–20% growth in commercial sales volume this year. Industrial and multifamily continue attracting the lion's share of capital while office faces record CMBS delinquencies above 12%.

items.headline: Agentic AI moves from pilot to operational dependence in proptech
items.summary: AI has shifted beyond experimentation into operational deployment, with agentic AI emerging as the next phase — autonomous agents now handle procurement, pricing, inventory, and event programming for real estate operators. Senior housing operators can deploy AI agents for staff scheduling, dynamic rent pricing, and predictive maintenance to combat margin pressure.

items.headline: Cedarhurst Senior Living advances multi-project development pipeline
items.summary: Cedarhurst Senior Living continues to keep its development and acquisition wheel turning with multiple projects underway in mid-2026, bucking the broader industry trend of thin pipelines. Provides a real-time benchmark for Lloyd Jones on construction costs, deal pacing, and operator expansion strategy.

items.headline: 10-year Treasury anchors near 4.1% even as Fed funds heads to 3%
items.summary: The Fed funds rate is expected to settle near 3% by late 2026, but the 10-year Treasury benchmark for most fixed-rate CRE loans has stayed stubbornly around 4.00–4.25% since mid-2025. Long-term financing costs are not falling as quickly as short-term rates, complicating refi math on stabilized senior housing assets.

items.headline: Assisted living construction costs climb in early 2026
items.summary: New assisted living construction costs are up early in 2026, while independent living costs remain relatively flat versus late 2025. Acquisitions become a more attractive growth path than ground-up development as cap rates compress and quality product comes to market.

items.headline: Proptech market projected to triple to $139B by 2033
items.summary: The proptech market is forecast to grow from $51.7 billion in 2026 to over $139 billion by 2033, with proptech investment hitting nearly $8.7 billion in the first nine months of 2025. AI valuation tools, fractional investing platforms, and smart-building IoT are the highest-momentum segments.

items.headline: 73% of business leaders expect revenue growth despite uncertainty
items.summary: JPMorgan's 2026 Business Leaders Outlook finds 73% anticipate revenue growth and 64% expect higher profits, though 49% flag uncertain economic conditions as their top concern. 58% plan to launch new products and 41% are doubling down on their most profitable offerings.

items.headline: Human + AI leadership defines 2026 management playbook
items.summary: DDI and World Business Forum identify human + AI leadership, flatter organizations, 'quiet cracking' burnout, and stalled internal mobility ('job hugging') as the dominant trends for 2026. Empathy and emotional intelligence are now table stakes for leaders running hybrid AI-human teams.

items.headline: 89% of employees say wellness directly boosts performance
items.summary: Wellhub's State of Work-Life-Wellness 2026 finds 89% of employees perform better when prioritizing wellbeing, with 44% citing lack of sleep as harmful to wellbeing and 40% pointing to work stress. Employers leaning into mental health support, flexible work, and preventive care are winning the retention battle.

items.headline: AI in Real Estate Summit convenes 300 leaders in NYC June 4
items.summary: The AI in Real Estate Summit runs June 4, 2026 in New York City during NY Tech Week, gathering 300 real estate and technology leaders to benchmark agentic AI adoption. Key showcase: AI valuation tools delivering faster, more accurate pricing than traditional appraisals.

items.headline: Senior living workforce and affordability top 2026 risk list
items.summary: Workforce, operational expenses, access and affordability, and the threat of increased regulation top industry leaders' concerns entering 2026, per McKnight's. Managing rising costs while recruiting and retaining staff is the structural challenge — labor-saving tech and disciplined expense management are the differentiators.

items.headline: Senior living monthly costs set to rise materially in 2026
items.summary: Newsweek reports senior living costs will rise materially in 2026, driven by wage pressure, insurance, and food inflation. The affordability gap continues to widen for middle-income seniors — an opening for operators with thoughtfully positioned middle-market product.

items.headline: Strategic localization replaces global-default operating model
items.summary: Leaders are redesigning operating models to be regional rather than global-default, building resilience closer to where value is created. For multi-region real estate operators, this translates to more market-by-market underwriting and decentralized decision rights.

items.headline: Emotional fitness emerges as new wellness category in 2026
items.summary: Dr. Axe and other 2026 wellness trend reports identify emotional fitness as a new daily-practice category alongside physical fitness, emphasizing cognitive performance and emotional resilience. For senior living operators, this trend reshapes activity programming and staff training expectations.

items.headline: Wellness 'third places' redefine workplace social fabric
items.summary: Employees are gravitating to gyms, yoga studios, and wellness hubs as 'third places' outside home and office, becoming new centers of professional relationship-building. Operators of mixed-use and senior communities can capitalize by integrating wellness amenities that double as social infrastructure.

items.headline: Hyper-personal wellness goes mainstream via AI coaches and biomarkers
items.summary: Wellness is becoming hyper-personal and data-driven, with biomarker-based nutrition, smart wearables tracking stress, and AI-powered wellness coaches delivering custom care. Senior housing operators piloting these tools can differentiate on resident health outcomes and family-facing reporting.

items.headline: Learning velocity becomes the new talent battleground
items.summary: Per World Business Forum's 2026 outlook, the talent competition has shifted from hiring to learning velocity — professionals expect growth opportunities as part of the offer, making L&D investment a core attraction and retention lever. Operators that institutionalize structured upskilling will outpace peers on staff stability.
